title: English Open World 1 – Unit 3 (Healthy living)

topics.title: Language Check – some/any & adverbs of sequence

theory.p: Use **some** in positive sentences. Use **any** in negative sentences and questions.
Same rule as some/any: something/somebody/somewhere vs anything/anybody/anywhere.
Forms with **no-** can be used (mostly in positive sentences).

choices: Have you got some sweets at home?
tasks.explain: Use **some** in positive sentences; **any** in questions/negative sentences.

tasks.q: Have you got ____ sweets at home?

tasks.explain: Correct form: Nobody is perfect.

tasks.q: Choose the correct: ____ oranges do you need?
tasks.explain: Oranges are countable → how many.

tasks.q: Rewrite this as a correct sentence (use your rules): We haven't got some milk.
alt: We have not got any milk.

tasks.q: Choose the correct: ____ oranges do you need?
tasks.explain: Oranges are countable → how many.

sections.title: L3.2 Adverbs of sequence (first, then, finally …)

theory.p: Adverbs of sequence show the order things happen: **first** (beginning), **next/then/after that** (following actions), **finally** (end).

tasks.q: ____, go to the end of the street, then turn left.
Choose the correct: ____ oranges do you need?
tasks.explain: Oranges are countable → how many.

tasks.q: Rewrite this as a correct sentence (use your rules): We haven't got some milk.
alt: We have not got any milk.

tasks.explain: Negative imperative: Do not + base form.

tasks.q: Put the adverbs of sequence in a logical order (beginning → end).
tasks.explain: First = beginning, then = middle, finally = end.

sections.title: L3.3 Countable vs uncountable + how much/how many

theory.p: Objects/people/things can be counted; use a/an and plural forms (e.g., a carrot, seven eggs).
Materials/abstract things: no plural form (e.g., water, meat, salt).
Use **how many** with countable nouns; **how much** with uncountable nouns.

choices: How much apples do you need?
How many apples do you need?
How many butter do you need?
tasks.explain: how many + countable (apples); how much + uncountable (butter).

tasks.q: How ____ butter do you need for the cake?

tasks.explain: Negative imperative: Do not + base form.
Negative imperative: Do not + base form.
Negative imperative: Do not + base form.
Negative imperative: Do not + base form.

tasks.q: Order the steps to ask correctly about amounts.
tasks.explain: Decide the noun type first, then choose the question word.

theory.p: Base form of verb + rest of sentence. Example: Cut the tomato into little cubes.
Do not + base form + rest. Example: Do not cut the small tomatoes.

tasks.hint: negative imperative: Do not + base form

tasks.q: Which sentence is a NEGATIVE imperative?
choices: Do not cut the small tomatoes.
Cut the tomato into little cubes.
tasks.explain: Negative imperative: Do not + base form.

items: Cut the tomato into little cubes.

topics.title: Study skills – preparing questions & statements
topics.intro: Tipps aus LC_26 (Interviewfragen und kurze Statements).

theory.p: Make your questions short and precise.
Closed questions → yes/no (e.g., Do you like sport?).
Use question words: what, where, when, why, how (often/much/many…).
Could you tell me more? Can you explain in more detail? Is there anything else?

choices: What do you like about playing football?
tasks.explain: Open questions use question words and invite longer answers.

tasks.q: Use question words like what, where, when, why, ____.
Rewrite this as a correct sentence (use your rules): We haven't got some milk.
alt: We have not got any milk.

theory.p: Make notes with keywords you want to use.
Use a dictionary for needed words (e.g., Fussballtrainer → football coach).
Prepare short statements you can say (e.g., I usually play football with my friend Diego).
Keep notes ready to add more details.

theory.p: I can understand details in interviews about sport. I can understand main points in a text about food. I can follow instructions (e.g., recipes).
I can discuss food topics (e.g., fast food). I can express likes/dislikes and agreement/disagreement. I can write short instructions (e.g., recipes).
I can use some/any correctly. I can use how much/how many correctly.
I can think about meanings/translations of food words and know the food words important for me.
I can use strategies for preparing short questions and statements.
